summary: McKenzie kicks off a 30-day sprint to build a new product: a widget-building platform for small businesses. He argues that while the Internet made spreading ideas cheap, the actual mechanics of sharing — linking — remain surprisingly hard for non-technical people. He proposes a service that lets businesses create embeddable widgets without programming expertise, combining data sources and presentations through a simple interface. The business model is subscription-based, with tiered pricing tied to feature access. He sets deliberately modest growth goals: one customer, then ten, then a hundred, then a few hundred paying subscribers — enough to support him indefinitely.
key_points: Linking is the Internet's foundational building block, but it's surprisingly difficult for non-technical users and limited in its expressiveness
Widgets represent an evolution beyond links — they carry their own reason for spreading and embed their own sharing mechanism
Most businesses can't create widgets because it requires web app development, databases, JavaScript, and hosting infrastructure
The proposed platform would let non-technical users snap together pre-coded building blocks to create widgets in minutes instead of paying thousands for custom development
A few hundred paying subscribers at $10-100/month would generate enough revenue to be sustainable, rejecting Silicon Valley-style growth fantasies
money_quotes: I pay about twenty two cents to get my software in front of people. If you had said that to an AOL exec in the nineties, he would have paid me a few hundred million and told me to print enough CDs to wallpaper every home in Africa.
After all that effort spent in getting your fans to care about you, we consistently fail at giving them the tools to show the world they care.
Its so foundational, so ingrained into the soul of us tech folks, that we forget how difficult and limiting it is.

And what did mankind come up with once it started embracing widgets? Well, a lot of Would You Survive The Zombie Apocalypse quizzes and What Kind of My Little Pony Are You badges. But there is the potential for so much more.

Now, if I were a Silicon Valley entrepeneur I would say 'We're looking at the potential of 100,000 customers by next year, giving us a valuation of $8.7 billion'. Happily, I don't live in Silicon Valley, and thus have more modest aspirations.
I'm going to start by getting myself a customer. Then I'm going to get 10. Then I'm going to get a hundred. And then I'm going to get a few hundred.
Its a lot like AdSense, except minus the ad blindness, the constricting limitations of that ad format, and, oh yeah, the cost.
key_insight: The Internet made idea-spreading cheap but left the actual mechanics of sharing — embedding rich, interactive content — inaccessibly hard for non-technical businesses, creating a market opportunity for a widget-building platform.
